content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of NNN integers. You can do the following two types of operations any (possibly zero) number of times:


Pick two indices iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤∣A∣,i≠j)(1 \leq i,j \leq |A|, i \neq j)(1≤i,j≤∣A∣,i=j). Change Aj:=Aj+AiA_j := A_j + A_iAj​:=Aj​+Ai​ and remove the ithi^{th}ith element from the array.
Pick an index iii (1≤i≤∣A∣)(1 \leq i \leq |A|)(1≤i≤∣A∣). Split AiA_iAi​ into two **positive** integers XXX and YYY such that X+Y=AiX+Y=A_iX+Y=Ai​. Remove the ithi^{th}ith element from the array and append elements XXX and YYY to the array.
Find the **maximum** number of **distinct** elements present in the array after performing any number of operations of the above two types.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The TTT test cases then follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the size of the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum number of **distinct** elements present in the array after performing any number of operations of the above two types.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible number of distinct elements after performing any sequence of the allowed operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: sum = 1+2+4 = 7. The largest k with k(k+1)/2 ≤ 7 is k = 3 (1+2+3 = 6). Hence answer 3.
Test case 2: sum = 1+1+3+4 = 9. Largest k with k(k+1)/2 ≤ 9 is also k = 3 (1+2+3 = 6). Hence answer 3.
Sum = 2. The maximum k satisfying k(k+1)/2 ≤ 2 is k = 1 (1). So only 1 distinct element can be achieved.
Sum = 16. k = 5 because 5·6/2 = 15 ≤ 16 and 6·7/2 = 21 > 16. Thus the maximum number of distinct elements is 5.

solution.approach: Both operations preserve the total sum S of the array. Therefore we can transform the initial array into any multiset of positive integers whose sum equals S. To maximise the number of distinct elements we need the largest possible size k of a set of distinct positive integers with sum <= S. The optimal set is {1,2,…,k}. Its sum is k(k+1)/2. Choose the maximum k such that k(k+1)/2 <= S. If there is leftover (S - k(k+1)/2 > 0) we can add it to the largest element k, keeping all numbers distinct. Hence the answer for each test case is k = floor((sqrt(8*S + 1) - 1) / 2).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case to compute the sum, O(1) to compute k. Overall O(T * N).
